---
title: "Bayesian Adaptation"
---

The murkml model's core innovation is that it can be quickly calibrated to a new site using a small number of local water samples. This process — called **Bayesian adaptation** — works by blending the model's existing global knowledge with site-specific observations. Think of it as the model saying: "I know a lot about how turbidity relates to SSC in general; now I'm adjusting my prediction scale to match what I actually see at your site." The Bayesian approach is more conservative than simple regression when data are sparse: it hedges toward the global prediction rather than over-fitting to a handful of potentially unrepresentative samples. The pages here show how much improvement users can expect as they collect more local samples, and which sites benefit most.

## Adaptation Curve

This is the key question for any practitioner considering the tool: how many field samples do I need to collect before the model is useful at my site? The x-axis shows the number of local grab samples provided; the y-axis shows median site-level R² across all holdout sites at that calibration count. Two adaptation strategies are compared — samples drawn randomly from across the record (best-case, information-rich) vs. samples drawn from the most recent period only (realistic for a new deployment). The OLS benchmark line shows what a traditional site-specific regression achieves with the same samples. The goal is to see CatBoost+adaptation cross and stay above the OLS line at low N.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-adaptation-curve
from pathlib import Path
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, ".")
from _scripts.figures import make_adaptation_curve

fig = make_adaptation_curve(Path("dashboard_data/summary.json"))
fig.show()
```

## How Shrinkage Works

When you have only 2–5 local samples, any single outlier — a storm event, a lab error, or a sensor glitch — can wildly distort a simple regression fit. Bayesian shrinkage (also called regularization) addresses this by treating local samples as evidence that updates a prior belief rather than as the sole truth. The parameter shown on the y-axis is the **effective weight given to local data** vs. global model knowledge. With few samples (left side), the weight stays low, so the global model dominates and is protected against over-fitting. As sample count grows (right side), local evidence earns more influence. The Student-t prior used here is heavier-tailed than a Gaussian, which means a single extreme sample has less ability to hijack the fit.

<!-- TODO: Verify the y-axis label on the weight curve figure matches the shrinkage weight definition in the adaptation code — cannot confirm without rendered output. -->

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-weight-curve
from _scripts.figures import make_bayesian_weight_curve

fig = make_bayesian_weight_curve()
fig.show()
```

## Zero-Shot Baseline by Site Quality

Before any local samples are added, the model still makes predictions — this is called **zero-shot** performance. The quality of that baseline varies enormously across sites: some sites are very well predicted out of the box because they resemble training sites; others are systematically off because their watershed or sensor characteristics are unusual. This figure shows three representative holdout sites — one that the global model handles well, one that is marginal, and one that is poorly predicted — illustrating the range of starting conditions that adaptation must overcome. Each panel shows observed SSC (x-axis) against predicted SSC (y-axis) at zero calibration samples.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-shrinkage-viz
from _scripts.figures import make_bayesian_shrinkage_viz

fig = make_bayesian_shrinkage_viz(
    Path("dashboard_data/per_reading.parquet"),
    Path("dashboard_data/summary.json"),
)
fig.show()
```

## Adaptation Surprise

Not all sites benefit equally from local calibration. Some sites that start with poor zero-shot predictions improve dramatically with just 10 samples (the adaptation "works"), while others that look reasonable at zero-shot barely improve (perhaps because the global model is already near-optimal there). The x-axis shows zero-shot site R² and the y-axis shows R² after adaptation with N=10 samples. Sites in the upper-left quadrant are the "surprise" cases: poorly predicted without calibration, but rescued by it. Sites near the diagonal received little benefit from adaptation. Understanding which site characteristics fall into each region can guide field sampling prioritization.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-adaptation-surprise
from _scripts.figures import make_adaptation_surprise

fig = make_adaptation_surprise(Path("dashboard_data/per_site.parquet"))
fig.show()
```
